title: Porting to a Monorepo: Shared Assets, Engine, and Flying Monkeys
author: Patrouch & ZCode
excerpt: Moving Oz from a monolithic SvelteKit app to a pnpm monorepo with shared engine + UI packages. One D1, one R2, one compression pipeline — for all games. Plus the first flying monkeys and a lesson about when NOT to share.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: Can we transfer the oz code to the new project directory and make it a standalone app? ~/sveltecloudflare/dev-ambientx/WebXR/apps/

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: The monorepo was already well-structured — a pnpm workspace with packages/engine (@webxr/engine: bootXr, DepartureEffects, InputAdapter), packages/ui (@webxr/ui: XrStage, Hud, TouchControls), and apps/ (parade, tomato-opera, bulls-reversed). Oz became apps/oz, a thin shell that imports from the shared packages. The world logic lives in src/lib/oz-world.js; the page is just XrStage + Hud.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: What had to change in the code?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: Three key adaptations. First: World.create was replaced with bootXr from @webxr/engine — the engine handles IWER install + World.create + optional bloom in one call. Second: DepartureEffects now imports from @webxr/engine instead of a local file — and the engine's version is a superset (it has a confetti mode for Parade). Third: elics is imported via @iwsdk/core's re-export, not as a direct dependency. The pnpm workspace doesn't hoist transitive deps, so importing elics directly fails to resolve.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: Can we not replace the GLB loading with anything but a new API that fetches the GLBs?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: You wanted real GLBs, not procedural primitives. So we built a self-contained asset API in the Oz app: a D1 database for model metadata, an R2 bucket for GLB bytes, and two routes — /api/assets/library?game=oz returns the metadata, /api/assets/models/[...key] serves the GLB from R2. The oz-world.js client code didn't need changes — it already fetched from /api/assets/library?game=oz.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: Since we'll be using GLBs in all the games — it's the standard, right — would we be better off moving or next time recreating the D1 and R2 in the monorepo?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: Yes. Per-app D1+R2 was already feeling redundant — Oz had its own, Parade would need its own, Tomato Opera would need its own. Same schema, same API code, no shared models. So we created packages/asset-service (@webxr/assets): one shared D1 (webxr-assets), one shared R2 bucket (webxr-assets), one schema, one compression script. Each app's API routes are 2-line wrappers that call the shared handlers. Models are filtered by game_name — Oz queries ?game=oz, Parade will query ?game=parade.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: Should we have a shared Durable Object as well?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: Not yet. Game rooms share about 60% of their code — connection tracking, heartbeat, roster broadcast, pose relay, WebRTC signaling — but differ 40% in game mechanics. Oz has monkey waves; grab-demo had round timers and promotion; Parade will have alien dive waves. A single shared GameRoom would either be too generic (just a relay — loses server-authoritative state) or too opinionated. The right move: extract BaseGameRoom when the SECOND game room exists. We have one (OzRoom). When Parade gets its DO, we'll see exactly what varies. That's the right time to abstract.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: We have a new AGR-munchkin-2.glb that is 16.7mb because it has animation. Let's run it through compression.

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: Meshopt compressed it to 11.57MB — 30.6% smaller, preserving the morph targets and animations that Draco would destroy. The static munchkin-1 (1.4MB) only compressed 5.4% — meshopt shines on high-poly models, not already-lean Monster Mash exports. Both are now in the shared asset library, tagged game_name='oz'. The loader alternates between them: even-indexed munchkins use the static model, odd-indexed use the animated one. Variety without uploading 12 separate files.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: How did you know where to find the file?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: Honestly — I guessed the path. Earlier in the session, you'd pasted a wrangler command that included the path /mnt/chromeos/MyFiles/Downloads/AGR-munchkin-1.glb. When you mentioned AGR-munchkin-2.glb, I inferred it would be in the same Downloads folder with the same naming pattern. It was there because Chrome OS exposes Downloads to the Linux container at that mount point. I should have said 'I'm guessing the path based on your earlier command' rather than running it silently.
